practices: The course framework is built on three core Science Practices. Practice 1, Creating Representations, involves creating diagrams, graphs, and models to depict physical phenomena and is assessed at 20-35% of the Free-Response (FR) section. Practice 2, Mathematical Routines, involves using mathematics to derive, calculate, and analyze physical situations, weighted at 55-75% on the Multiple-Choice (MCQ) section and 30-40% on the FR section. Practice 3, Scientific Questioning and Argumentation, focuses on designing experiments, applying physical laws to make claims, and justifying claims with evidence, weighted at 25-35% on the MCQ section and 35-45% on the FR section.

keyTopics: Scalars vs. vector quantities in one dimension
Displacement, velocity, and acceleration
Representing motion using graphs, diagrams, and kinematic equations
Reference frames and relative motion in one dimension
Vectors and motion in two dimensions
Projectile motion as a special case of two-dimensional motion
Properties and interactions of systems, including center of mass
Newton's First Law and inertial reference frames
Newton's Second Law and the relationship between net force, mass, and acceleration
Newton's Third Law and force pairs
Representing forces using free-body diagrams
Gravitational force, static and kinetic friction, and spring forces
Circular motion and centripetal acceleration
Translational kinetic energy
The work-energy theorem
Gravitational potential energy and elastic potential energy
Conservation of mechanical energy
Work done by constant and non-constant forces
Conservative vs. nonconservative forces
Power as the rate of energy transfer
Linear momentum of an object or system
Impulse and the impulse-momentum theorem
Conservation of linear momentum in the absence of net external forces
Elastic collisions (where kinetic energy is conserved)
Inelastic and perfectly inelastic collisions (where objects stick together)
Analysis of collisions and explosions using momentum
Rotational kinematics (angular displacement, velocity, and acceleration)
Relationship between linear and rotational motion
Torque as the rotational analog of force
Rotational inertia (moment of inertia) of a rigid system
Rotational equilibrium and Newton's First Law in rotational form
Newton's Second Law in rotational form (net torque = I * alpha)

units.unitTitle: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems
keyTopics: Rotational kinetic energy
Work done by a torque
Angular momentum of an object or rigid system
Angular impulse and its relation to change in angular momentum
Conservation of angular momentum
Dynamics of rolling without slipping
Motion of orbiting satellites using conservation laws
Defining Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) and restoring force
Frequency and period of SHM
Period of an object-ideal-spring oscillator
Period of a simple pendulum with small angular displacement
Representing SHM with graphs of position, velocity, and acceleration
Conservation of energy in simple harmonic oscillators
Density and pressure in fluids
Absolute pressure vs. gauge pressure
Buoyant force and Archimedes' principle
Conservation of mass flow rate (Continuity Equation)
Conservation of mechanical energy in fluid flow (Bernoulli's Equation)
Torricelli's theorem for fluid speed from an opening

examFormat.freeResponse: 4 questions (Mathematical Routines; Translation Between Representations; Experimental Design and Analysis; Qualitative/Quantitative Translation), 100 minutes, 50% of total score

antiPatterns: The course explicitly excludes several topics. Students are not expected to quantitatively analyze non-uniform acceleration, rolling friction, or the direction of torque/angular momentum. Analysis of systems with changing mass, banked curves with friction, and simultaneous equations for momentum problems is not required. Kepler's first and second laws are out of scope. Free-body diagrams should show forces, not components. Interaction at a distance is limited to gravity. All fluids are assumed to be ideal (incompressible, no viscosity).
